data.title: Amarin expects $70M in operating expense savings by June 30, 2026 while targeting positive 2026 cash flow
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amarin Corporation plc (AMRN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The momentum that started to build in late 2025 continued in the first quarter of 2026" (CEO, President & Director Aaron Berg), adding that "our results and cash generation in the quarter demonstrate our progress in advancing our new business model" and that Amarin has "substantially completed our previously announced global restructuring."
  • "We remain on track to achieve the estimated $70 million in total operating expense savings by June 30, 2026" (CEO, President & Director Berg), while also highlighting balance sheet positioning: "Our cash balance of $308 million rose from year-end 2025" and "we reported a second consecutive quarter of positive cash flow and ended the quarter with no debt."
  • "Our growth engine is comprised of a fully partnered international commercial strategy that's anchored by our exclusive license and supply agreement with Recordati" (CEO, President & Director Berg), and "as of March 31, 2026, Recordati had commenced sales of VAZKEPA in 10 countries" with European revenue described as having "rose significantly from Q4 2025."
  • "While our U.S. franchise continues to see revenue declines due to the pressures of generic competition, VASCEPA remains the clear U.S. market leader" (CEO, President & Director Berg), with additional market share commentary: "Our share of the market rose to 48% at March 31, 2026, up from 42% in the same period last year" and "VASCEPA branded prescriptions rose by 17% in Q1 2026 versus Q1 2025."
  • "Total net revenue in Q1 2026 rose to $45.1 million from $42 million in last year's first quarter" (Senior VP & CFO Peter Fishman), and "we generated positive cash flow from operations of $6.4 million in the first quarter" while reiterating: "we expect to generate positive cash flow for 2026."

Outlook

  • "We expect to keep [exclusive contracts] through the rest of 2026" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman), while also cautioning that "if there are changes, that would have an impact there."
  • "We are confident that as we've turned into a cash flow positive position that, that will continue into the future" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman), adding that this is "driven by how we retain those exclusive contracts."
  • "Our partnered model will result in revenue variability quarter-to-quarter" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman), tying variability to "launch timing, end market demand and the structure of individual partnership agreements."

Financial Results

  • "Total net revenue in Q1 2026 rose to $45.1 million" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman), and "first quarter product revenue in Europe was $4.9 million" alongside "Rest of World revenue in Q1 2026 was $2.8 million."
  • "Cost of goods sold rose by $10.5 million or 62%" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman), and management said the company "expect[s] our cost of goods sold to continue to be higher until Q3 2026" due to the anniversary effect tied to regaining a U.S. exclusive PBM relationship.
  • "Total operating expenses declined by 31% or $12.8 million to $29.1 million" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman); "excluding the restructuring charge of $3.3 million, total operating expenses of $25.8 million declined by 38% from last year's first quarter."
  • "Our operating loss in the first quarter narrowed to $11.3 million" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman), and "we ended the quarter with cash and investments of $308 million, up from $303 million at year-end 2025."

Q&A

  • Jessica Fye, JPMorgan Chase & Co: "talk about the right way to think about the trend in U.S. net price" and cash flow durability; Senior VP & CFO Fishman responded, "the bulk of our year-over-year change occurs in Q1" and "we are confident" cash flow positivity can continue, while emphasizing it is "driven by how we retain those exclusive contracts."
  • Jessica Fye, JPMorgan Chase & Co: asked about gross margin lumpiness and SG&A run rate; CEO, President & Director Berg said gross margin "will be in part dependent on the supply shipments to our partners" and that excluding restructuring, "this is a good way to look at that ongoing run rate," while adding restructuring should be "a pretty nominal amount" in Q2 and "lower" than Q1.
  • Kyuwon Choi, Goldman Sachs: asked about guideline-driven physician behavior; CEO, President & Director Berg said feedback is "very positive" but "it will take time" to see impact, and Executive VP, President of Research & Development and Chief Scientific Officer Steven Ketchum added the guidelines "position icosapent ethyl as an important consideration."
  • Kyuwon Choi, Goldman Sachs: asked about returning cash to shareholders; CEO, President & Director Berg said buybacks or "cash back to shareholders" are "being discussed" but the company is thinking "more holistically, strategically about the total value of the company" alongside work with "our exclusive adviser, Barclays."
  • Byunghyun Ahn, Leerink Partners: asked about an authorized generic and rest-of-world momentum/milestones; CEO, President & Director Berg said Amarin does not believe "it's in our best interest to launch an authorized generic at this time" and would consider it "once we find we can't compete any longer with this strategy," while Senior VP & CFO Fishman said the company has been "pleased with the end market demand growth" but "haven't been providing specific guidance" on milestones.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, centering on durability of U.S. pricing/exclusives and cash flow, margin/expense cadence, and capital return, with direct prompts like "sustainable beyond '26?" (Jessica Fye, JPMorgan Chase & Co) and questions on buybacks (Kyuwon Choi, Goldman Sachs).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using confidence language including "we remain on track" (CEO, President & Director Berg) and "we are confident" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman), while repeatedly tying durability to exclusives retention.
  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1 commentary added more execution-focused momentum and cash-flow continuity language ("second consecutive quarter") alongside similar emphasis on a partnered Europe model and payer exclusives.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The Europe narrative shifted from transition framing in Q4 ("commenced in Q3 2025") to early rollout specifics in Q1, including that Recordati "had commenced sales of VAZKEPA in 10 countries" (CEO, President & Director Berg) and that Europe revenue "more than doubled" sequentially (Senior VP & CFO Fishman).
  • U.S. exclusivity remained central in both quarters, with Q1 adding explicit market share language ("share of the market rose to 48%") while Q4 focused on confidence to "maintain them through the year" (CEO, President & Director Berg).
  • Q4 discussed restructuring progress as "approximately half" achieved by year-end (CEO, President & Director Berg), while Q1 emphasized being "substantially completed" and reiterated the same June 30, 2026 savings timeline.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Our U.S. franchise continues to see revenue declines due to the pressures of generic competition" (CEO, President & Director Berg), with management’s mitigation centered on payer access: "we expect to maintain our exclusives with key payers through the end of 2026."
  • "Our partnered model will result in revenue variability quarter-to-quarter" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman), with drivers including supply shipment timing and demand across partner markets.
  • Management flagged that potential changes in exclusives would matter: "if there are changes, that would have an impact" (Senior VP & CFO Fishman).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as continued traction in a two-part model—U.S. cash generation under an exclusives strategy and international expansion via partners—while reiterating it is "on track" to deliver $70 million of operating expense savings by June 30, 2026 and maintaining expectations for positive cash flow in 2026, with longer-term cash flow and capital return discussions repeatedly tied to sustaining U.S. formulary exclusives and growing partner-led demand outside the U.S.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Meta's increased capex, lackluster guidance spook investors
data.description:
Meta Platforms (META) beat on both the top- and bottom-line in the first quarter but issued lackluster forward guidance and raised its capex expectations by a significant amount in anticipation of higher component pricing and increased data center costs.

Also weighing on shares is a continued loss in its Reality Labs business of $4.03B. The company also realized a quarter-over-quarter decline in family daily active people ((DAP)) attributed to internet disruptions in Iran and restricted access to WhatsApp in Russia.

Meta’s other metrics, however, showed impressive gains from the same quarter last year, with revenue increasing 33% to $56.3B (versus $55.5B estimates) as advertising revenue increased 33% to $55B and Family of Apps revenue gained 34% to $55.9B, both of which beat expectations.

On the bottom line, Meta (META) earned a profit of $10.44 per share, up 62% year-over-year and nearly $4 better than anticipated.

Looking ahead to the current quarter, despite a more than 30% increase in revenue for the first quarter, Meta Platforms (META) expects Q2 revenue to be within a range of $58B to $61B with a midpoint of $59.5B that is in-line with expectations.

Full-year 2026 expenses are expected to be in the range of $162B to $169B, unchanged from the prior outlook.

However, the company now expects to spend $125B to $145B in capital expenditures, up from the prior range of $115B to $135B.

Reflecting on the results, Seeking Alpha analyst Steven Fiorillo called this a “drop the mic moment for META as DAPs increased 4% YoY and Ad impressions increased 19% YoY. This led to META producing $56.31 billion in revenue, an increase of 33% YoY, and $26.77 billion in net income, which was up 60% YoY. META being down because the market is scared of its CapEx guidance boost is missing the bigger picture.”

“Markets seem skeptical of META’s increased CapEx guidance, but META’s bread and butter keeps growing,” added Seeking Alpha analyst Geneva Investor. “I see META as a global market leader in digital advertising, now at an even deeper discount against its direct peer Alphabet…. the AI CapEx investment represents a potential future upside, but META is worth a buy at these prices just based on its ad business, and even if we don't see any AI returns in the short term.”

data.title: Moelis Non-GAAP EPS of $0.11 misses by $0.42, revenue of $319.8M misses by $3M
data.description:
• Moelis press release (MC): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.11 misses by $0.42.
  • Revenue of $319.8M (+4.3% Y/Y) misses by $3M.
  • First quarter 2026 Adjusted pre-tax margin of 15.0% vs. 14.0% in the prior year period
  • Continued to execute on our growth strategy:
    • Since our last earnings release, one Managing Director focused on private credit secondaries and one focused on Healthcare have joined the Firm
    • Six additional Managing Directors are committed to join throughout the year, including two hires in Capital Markets, two hires in London focused on Chemicals and Sponsors, and two hires in Energy and Private Capital Advisory
  • Strong balance sheet with cash and short-term investments of $353.7 million and no debt
    • Declared regular quarterly dividend of $0.65 per share
    • Repurchased 1.9 million shares during the first quarter, including a quarterly record 0.9 million open market repurchases at an average price of $59.78 per share
    • Including the dividend declared today, we will have returned $171.4 million to shareholders with respect to the first quarter of 2026

data.title: Plexus announces CFO transition plan
data.description:
• Plexus (PLXS) announced chief financial officer Patrick Jermain will retire.
  • David Abuhl has been appointed SVP and CFO, effective May 11, 2026.
  • Abuhl currently serves as SVP-Finance and joined the leadership team in 2025.
  • Jermain will remain in an advisory role through July 31, 2026, to support transition.
